videos.title: 6 Tourist Pitfalls in JERUSALEM (and how to avoid them..)
videos.description:
For all my tips, accommodation recommendations, tours, booklets and app, check out my internet site - www.travelingisrael.com

For all my tips, accommodation recommendations, digital tours and booklets, check out my internet site - https://www.travelingisrael.com/shop-products/

Like my content? Want to support my work? Become a member and get early access to my videos as well as other perks!
Support me on Ko - fi - https://ko-fi.com/travelingisrael

0:00 - Intro scammers in Jerusalem
0:50 - Mount of Olives lookout 
1:30 - Outside Gethsemane Church
2:03 - Via Dolorosa
2:25 - Restaurant tricks 
3:40 - The red string
4:20 - Jaffa Gate
5:02 - Heaven & hell..


---
Follow me..
ko-fi - https://ko-fi.com/travelingisrael
Facebook - https://www.facebook.com/travelingisrael
Instergam - https://www.instagram.com/travelingisrael/
Tiktok - https://www.tiktok.com/@travelingisrael1
videos.transcript: hi everyone as you know Jerusalem has many holy sites I've made a lot of videos about them but what you need to know is that in between the holy sites there are quite a few non-holy people who may try to scam you in this video I will show you where you should be extra careful and I will give you some very useful tips to avoid these scammers I will say that Jerusalem is safer for tourists than most other cities in Berlin I saw much more sophisticated ways to scam tourists but you know still nobody wants to visit a religious site and get scammed two minutes later it can ruin the whole day by the way I see a deep connection between these scammers who want an extra dollar and the holy sites I will talk about it at the end of the video but now the tactless well should you be more alert many tours especially group tours start the day at the top of the amount of Olives my Jerusalem booklet does too you have the sun behind you the Magnificent old city of Jerusalem in front of you and whole loads of scam artists all around you in short I will say that this is not the place to buy that fake Rolex watch you've always wanted make sure you keep your wallet closed if you want to change your lens on your camera then go to a corner to do it or enter the cemetery and do it there from the lookout most Travelers continue to Gethsemane outside the church there are some very aggressive sellers I guess it is their right to be aggressive but it is also your right not to buy my tip here is simple if you feel pressured then take it as a sign you shouldn't buy anything from them ask what the prices before you touch the object and don't pick anything up in your hand The next place you should be aware of is the Via Dolorosa. Now there are dozens of shops along the Via Dolorosa, and most of them are decent Merchants who want to make a living but among them there are some very aggressive sellers remember compare the prices from one shop to another and never put up with ugly Behavior so we are probably hungry by now in my Jerusalem app I pinpoint some great options but let's say that you've made a mistake and came to Jerusalem without purchasing my app with all my tours and tips it happens people make mistakes anyway there are some restaurants that play a dirty trick on customers in restaurants you should always ask for the menu if you get a menu without a prices list it's a very bad sign if they don't let you know what the prices are then you should be suspicious don't even order a cup of coffee they will charge you 10 bucks for it now there is another thing that they do they they give you a menu and you see a Falafel or Shawarma and the price is reasonable and you order but then they bring you the Falafel plate which is much more expensive now in many restaurants in Israel you can buy falafelina pita bread or in a plate which is a bit more expensive it is very common but in some of the restaurants they charge a ridiculous price for the Falafel plate without asking you so my tip for you here is to get my app and if not say that you want a Falafel for seven dollars and not the plate for 25 bucks to be aware of on your way to the Western Wall from the Jewish quarter you will come across people wanting to tie a red string around your wrist and bless you and then they will ask you for money if you want to donate money great time for it but I don't like people preferring other people to donate money and I don't know who the money goes to my tip for you here don't accept the red string and just keep walking The last place I want to show you is where most tourists enter the city Jaffa Gate. Here I wouldn’t change money, or use the services of any of the illegal guides who will just take you to their uncles’ shops and pressure you to buy jewelry, or use any taxis. Jaffa Gate is the main gateway into the city so there are plenty of honest shops and the great tip-based tours start from here. But in general, be aware. Now I have to say that I know that these kinds of videos are very helpful for travelers but for me personally, as a tour guide, I would rather talk about history and religion so let’s add a bit of extra knowledge to this video. Shall we? The scammers are not a new thing. They were here from the moment Jerusalem became Jerusalem. Mark Twain wrote about most of what I’ve outlined here 150 years ago. The best-selling of Twain’s work during his lifetime was the travel book “The Innocents Abroad”, which he wrote while he was here in 1867. His book, not so politically correct I must say, describes Jerusalem better than any travel video you will ever see. Including, of course, my videos. But there is another point to make here: how can it be that next to the holiest places – the place where the Jewish temples stood, where Jesus was resurrected, where Muhammed ascended to heaven to negotiate with God about how many prayers a Muslim ought to pray – right next to these places that are so important to billions of people, you have someone trying to rip you off and sell you a falafel for $25. The highest and the lowest are two sides of the same coin. Think about it, the Jewish temples stood where Abraham almost sacrificed his son, and where Jesus suffered on the cross, next to thieves, humiliated, mocked by the masses. Sacrifice, humiliation, blood, holiness, faith… These are all very closely related. Here, on this side, are the holiest sites and here, just outside Jaffa Gate is the valley, Gay in Hebrew, where they sacrificed children. Guy Ben Hinom, Gehenom, hell. It is not a coincidence that the stairway to heaven and hell are so close. That’s it, if you want my personal blessing then give this video a like, subscribe, check out my internet site and be a good person. See you in Jerusalem. Yalla bye.

safetyNotes: 6 Tourist Pitfalls in JERUSALEM (and how to avoid them

Traveling Israel - https://www.travelingisrael.com/

videos.transcript: is traveling to Israel safe the answer is yes I know that after everything you have heard in the news about Israel for the last year and a half it is hard to visualize Israel as a tourist destination at at the moment however if you have the chance visiting now might actually be a really good idea because there are far fewer tourists in this video I will be showing you the main places you will be spending time as a tourist in Jerusalem so you can see for yourself how calm and peaceful the streets are so in this video I won't be taking you to the famous sites so know Western Wall or the Church of the Holy SRE I've done that in lots of other videos today I will be showing you the area with all the hotels and restaurants I want to give you an impression of daily life here so you can decide for yourself if this is the time to visit I will start with safety because when it comes to traveling that is always people's top concern as a tourist you will be safe and feel safe many Americans and Europeans have told me that walking in Jerusalem or Tel Aviv at night is much safer than in most big cities in their countries ask anyone who has been to Israel and I doubt you will find anyone who says that they didn't feel safe what I hear from all the tourists I guide is that they might have worried about safety before they came but once they were here those Warriors disappeared and I will say that although Jerusalem is complicated when it comes to history and politics traveling in Israel especially if you come for a few days let's say five six days is very simple and easy both Jerusalem and tviv are tiny cities so you can get almost everywhere on foot which in my opinion adds Great Value to the experience and no don't worries about any mountains even though you've read about the Temple Mount and the Mount of Olives they aren't really mountains just beautiful Hills that are easily walkable to get specific on itineraries I would suggest three nights in Jerusalem and two nights in Tel Aviv on one of your days in Jerusalem you can easily take a tour to Madan the Dead Sea which is a must you can rent a car take an organized tour or a private tour Mada is about 1 and a half hours driveway you might also choose to stay a whole week in Tel Aviv and take the trend to Jerusalem or join a Day Tour to Jerusalem there are also tours to msada in the Dead Sea from Tel Aviv when you come to Jerusalem you will probably start here the train station is down there so if you arrived by train from the airport you will come up right here here is the Light Rail station and the bus station is inside this building let me add that in Tel Aviv the area around the central bus station is not a good area so try to avoid it after dark but here in Jerusalem it is perfectly fine daytime and nighttime what I like as an Israeli who doesn't live in Jerusalem is that every time I come here I'm like oh I'm in Jerusalem now as non- Israelis you can't really see it but Israelis can look at someone and from their closes from the kind of yulka they were or from their hat or general dress which religious group and which political party they belong to from here I will take the light rail two stations to the muda market and walk from there to my hotel since I'm going to be recommending that you book a hotel in that area as you will quickly learn the Israelis haven't yet learned that first you let people get off the train and then you get on the train although we are God's chosen people we are far from perfect social niceties such as thank you you're welcome please as well as the concept of personal space are all precious Commodities so we don't use them too often so here we have the market there are many places to eat here but I always opt for Sab at AR SA sabik is a PA full of all the good stuff Egg Eggplant potato salad Kina this Market is mainly for Israelis so don't worry about the sellers ripping you off the only places you should be um bit wary of are the Spice shops customers pay per 100 gram and they fill lots of small bags for you really quickly and it can get very pricey A good rule of thumb is that if a seller approach in English you would better keep walking I like to buy my spices here at mrai they have loads of great stuff and if you are looking to take something home with you they have plenty of good things for you to choose from from the market I will continue along Jaffer road which is the main road that leads to the Old City here is the junction with hanim Street Jaffa Road was historically the Commercial Street and hanim the prophet Street was the one with all the institutions I do recommend walking along here and having a look at the houses here is Zion square and here you have the Ben Huda pedestrian street as I said earlier I recommend choosing a hotel or hostel in this area so in one of the streets starting at the market and ending at the city hall light rail station I'm staying here at the betal hotel it is a fantastic location close to the market okay so this is my room I have a bed also a shower toilet there is also a sink and a mirror I think that my videos are valuable source of information however there are travel vloggers who do better hotel reviews than me one of the things that people love about Israel is the food the street food is excellent but there are also plenty of restaurants that have a lot of character and atmosphere the service is always really friendly and informal the music can be loud and in my experience most tourists like it but for those who just want to have a quiet meal after a long day of sing there are also plenty of places to go to I'm now going to show you a restaurant called muda This is a bit more extreme um when it comes to the music but I want to give you like an impression so you will know what it looks [Applause] like I hope this City Walk has given you a teste of what you will encounter in in the streets of Jerusalem below the video I will be leaving a link to my website with all my tips on planning your trip accommodation recommendations and much more now if you go to my website you will also see that I'm selling my Jerusalem digital tours and on my videos I promote my Jerusalem course the content doesn't vary that much the digital tours are made for those who are actually traveling to Jerusalem they will come in very useful once you are here on a map you can see where you are how to walk to the next stop and at every stop you can listen to my explanation on the site in front of you trust me you need the tours because the sites in Jerusalem are not that impressive in themselves you have to know the story behind them the Jerusalem Crush course on the other hand is for those who want to enjoy Jerusalem from their own home in Wisconsin or any other part of the world it is where I kind of bring Jerusalem to you of of course you can also use it for inspiration or as preparation for a trip and actually I think that you might want to come when you have watched it so you can enjoy the magic firsthand I also added a 10% discount code for all the Abram hostels and tours which I also really like to recommend so if you on a budget or just don't want to spend too much money on accommodation my discount code will save you more than the cost of the digital tours as you might have sense by now I feel strongly about Jerusalem and I want you to experience this city in one way or the other the thing about Jerusalem is that it is more than just a city with important sites the sites in Jerusalem are all about the core values of our society all the links will be here below please like And subscribe yala come to Jerusalem see you next week yala bye for

videos.title: Jerusalem’s Best Foods (and What to Avoid): The Ultimate Food Guide
videos.description:
The best food in Jerusalem!
If Jerusalem is on your travel list, my tours will help you make the most of your time there. You’ll get clear explanations of the city’s most important sites, my personal map with food and shopping recommendations, and discount codes for hostels I trust in Tel Aviv, Jerusalem, and Eilat. Full details on my website - https://www.travelingisrael.com/shop-products/

All the places I mentioned in the video: Gagou De Paris, Kadosh Café Patisserie, Abu Kamel, LIna, Rova coffee house, Austrian Hospice, Notre Dame of Jerusalem Center, Mona, Chakra, Mizrachi - HaShaked st. Morduch, Rachmo, Azura, Dwiny Pita Bar, Arica sabich, Bourekas Ramle, Bourekas Haifa, Arais, Aka, Halati, Nuna mufleta, Joni kubaneh, Mousseline Jerusalem, Machneyuda.

---
Follow me...
ko-fi - https://ko-fi.com/travelingisrael
Facebook - https://www.facebook.com/travelingisrael
Instagram - https://www.instagram.com/travelingisrael/
Tiktok - https://www.tiktok.com/@travelingisrael1
videos.transcript: If you are planning a visit to Jerusalem, you are going to love this video. I'm going to show you some of the places I love to eat in Jerusalem and the places I love recommending to tourists. Before I started eating all day, I stopped by Yehuda Bakery in the city center for a let's call it a pre-breakfast snack. Let's start with breakfast. I like to begin a tour in the Old City as early as possible. Inside the Old City, I don't really have breakfast recommendations. So, I prefer to start here at Kadosh Cafe. A classic Israeli breakfast is usually cheeses, eggs, and salad, no meat. Partly because of kashrut and partly because even Israelis who don't keep kashrut still think meat for breakfast is weird. And at Kadosh, they've got great pastries and croissants. From here, we will head into the Old City. Two more excellent breakfast options are Menashe Cafein and Cafe Yehoshua. They are just a bit further from the main tourist route, but they are also highly recommended. In the Old City of Jerusalem, people mostly eat hummus. I usually eat at Abu Kamel Ramzi. It's a bit hard to find because it's inside a shop in a shuk market, but you can always ask. Another recommended place is Lina Hummus. Almost every time I film in the Old City, I sit here at the Hava Coffee House. It's quiet. I love the atmosphere. Most tourists and groups get sucked into the food court area. It's like over there. And here, it is much calmer. Jerusalem is a very intense city, and here here you can breathe. On the way to the next place, we're going to pass the Western Wall. I stop here for a couple of reasons. First, whenever I'm in the Old City, I stop at the wall. I touch the stones and I tell myself, "This is for my grandfather's grandfather's grandfathers." For hundreds of years Jews dreamt about Jerusalem and we are lucky enough to live in a generation where Jerusalem is our capital again. Second, I told my son's kindergarten I will be at the Kotel, Western Wall, and they prepared little notes with their wishes. So, I'm delivering them. Also, a great way to virtue signaling that I'm a modern involved dad. And one more small thing. Food tours are huge trend now among Israelis. When we get to Machaneh Yehuda Market, you will see tons of Israeli food tours. It's fun, it's great, but I always think to myself, "You're in Jerusalem, you're in the most interesting city in the world, the most important place for the Jewish people. So, you ate khachapuri and you ate ara'yes and you ate sabich and you drank arak with lemon and you ate bourekas. Great. But, what about giving something small to your soul? And it doesn't have to be the Western Wall. Maybe a visit to the North Africa Jewish Heritage Center, which I made a video about, about the connection between North African Jews and Jerusalem. Tomorrow morning I'm filming at Ketef Hinom, a video in memory of Gabi Barkay who found the silver amulet with the priestly blessing in ancient Hebrew from 2,600 years ago. Or maybe an hour at the Temple Mount Sifting Project, where Gabi Barkay helped save soil from the Temple Mount, the holiest place for the Jewish people. You come up to Jerusalem, eat well, great, but give 1 hour to the soul, too. Now we're at the entrance to the Austrian Hospice. It's on the Via Dolorosa, near station number three. If the door is closed, you have to ring the bell and eventually they will open. I love this place, an Austrian island in the middle of the Muslim Quarter. Why do the Austrians have a hotel in the middle of the old city? Because of a war, a polite war. Towards the end of the 19th century, the Ottoman Empire was weakening. And after centuries when Europeans had almost no foothold in Jerusalem, a competition started. Who would build more and bigger? The Russians built the Russian compound, the Italians built, the French, the Germans. The Germans, of course, exaggerated. Across from the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, they built a church that is taller than the Holy Sepulchre. Like, really? Some respect. But, you know how Germans and wars. They always like to exaggerate. So, here the Austrians built a hospice. You can drink coffee and eat apple strudel while Emperor Franz Joseph watches you from the walls. They also have a beautiful garden. And I want to show you something cool. This table is a 2,000-year-old capital. There aren't many places in the world where your coffee table might have been once part of the temple, or Herod's palace, or the place where Pontius Pilate judged Jesus. Here you can see Ali Baba shop, one of my favorite souvenir shops in in the old city. And what I like about this shop is that they are fair. The price is fixed and you can see it on the item, and it is not decided based on your nationality and your face. I know. Genius. We've left the old city through the New Gate, and here there is a French compound. Why is there a French compound in Jerusalem? You already know the answer. It is called Notre Dame. And if you go in and go up to the fourth floor, there is a really good restaurant with crazy view. Better to reserve a seat. It's without question one of my favorite places. Some people can stay awake all day. I'm not one of them. And I never understood the obsession. You don't get a medal for making it to the end of the day. So, why not sleep? I cannot stay awake after lunch, so I'm going to take a nap, and I want to show you where I'm taking a room at a Post Hostel, very close to City Hall Railway Station and the Old City. This basic room cost me 350 shekels. You don't usually get prices like this for a private room. Now, you're probably thinking, "Okay, you pay less because you're a famous YouTuber." And the truth is, no. You will actually pay less than me because I'm Israeli and I have to pay 18% VAT, and as tourists, you don't pay that. I did use a coupon code, though. If you want the coupon code, buy my digital tours. In my tours, I filmed everything I say on my guided tours. You see where you're on the map, where the next stop is, and my explanations. And now I'm going to attach to that tours the recommendations map with all the restaurants, special shops, and a coupon code for Abraham Hostels in Tel Aviv and Eilat, for Abraham Tours, also their tours to Jordan, for the Spot Hostel in Tel Aviv, and for this hostel, Post Hostel. And one more thing, if you book on a computer, you get around 15% off, and if you book on your phone, it is more like 20%. If you're planning to come to Jerusalem, it is worth buying my tours even if you don't listen to a single word I say. The coupon codes alone can save you more money than the tours cost. Everything I've shown you so far is cheap to mid-range. If you want to treat yourself to a fancy dinner in the evening, you've got a few great options. I went to Mona, located in a beautiful building that used to be the first home of the Bezalel Academy of Arts. And after eating at upscale restaurant, I went to another, Chakra. Because why eat at one fancy restaurant if you can eat at two? For these restaurants, you need to book in advance. And on the map, I will add a few more options as well. At Machaneh Yehuda Market, there are tons of amazing places to eat. But before I start with the recommendations, a quick warning. Everyone loves the market, Israelis and tourists. And because of that, there are a few tourist traps. A good rule of thumb, if someone is aggressively pitching you stuff in English, treat it like a warning sign. In my experience, like the two types of shops tourists complain about the most are halva shops and like the tea spices shops. Their routine is always the same. They give you a tiny taste, you say, "Wow, this is great." And boom, suddenly you are walking away with a bag of tea that cost 350 shekels. So, like I don't know, $70. So, pay attention. Before you agree to just a little, ask for the price and say say no. You don't want to say say no. If you want to buy a few genuine good things to take home, I recommend Mizrahi, one of the oldest shops in the market. This is where I shop. One small warning though, if you buy these nuts here, do not open them. The moment you do, you won't stop eating. As for food, I'm going to start with the classics, the long-established spots. My top three, maybe I forgot someone, are Morduch, Rachmo, and Azura. These are places that have been serving the same food for 50 years. Not trendy food, not gimmicks, just big pots of great food. Stuffed vegetables, kubbeh, rice, meat. I'm not the type who says, "You must eat here." But these are the places that basically built the whole food scene in the shuk, in the market, and I really recommend them. When it comes to newer places, the one I sit at the most is the Vinny Street Food and Right. This is a place where they put everything into a pita, and there is something very Israeli about it. Very on the go. Just give it to me in a pita. And if I don't feel like sitting down, I eat sabich at Tal Bashan. Other recommended spots are Bureka Ramli and Bureka Haifa. Both are excellent, in my opinion. If you want meat, there is Araisu. Shawarma Akko is also very good, and so is Halati. Nona Mufleta and Johnny Cubana are the trendy places right now, and they are solid. And here at the end, I will say something controversial, but I stand behind it. When it comes to desserts, and desserts are close to my heart, I can recommend Mousseline ice cream, or you can go into Mahane Yehuda restaurant for dessert. I'm not that excited about the colorful cookie places and the knafeh stands. All right, guys, there are still tons of great places, but I literally cannot eat another bite. Everything I mentioned will be listed down below under the video. And if you want all my recommendations, the digital tours, and the coupon codes, grab my digital tours on my website. The link is right here below. See you in the next video. Yalla, bye.

videos.title: Traveling to Jerusalem? 3-day itinerary put together by a professional guide
videos.description:
Traveling to Jerusalem? Then this video is for you! There’s a lot to see in Jerusalem so I’m going to break down the planning process for three days in the city: which sites to see, how to organize your day, which sites you need to register for in advance and some very useful tips. 
For all the information click here - 
https://www.travelingisrael.com/jerusalem-travel-guide/

For all my tips, accommodation recommendations, digital tours and booklets, check out my internet site - https://www.travelingisrael.com/shop-products/

Like my content? Want to support my work? Become a member and get early access to my videos as well as other perks!
Support me on Ko - fi - https://ko-fi.com/travelingisrael
---
Follow me..
ko-fi - https://ko-fi.com/travelingisrael
Facebook - https://www.facebook.com/travelingisrael
instergam - https://www.instagram.com/travelingisrael/
tiktok - https://www.tiktok.com/@travelingisrael1
videos.transcript: Traveling to Jerusalem? Then this video is for you! There’s a lot to see in Jerusalem so I’m going to break down the planning process for three days in the city: which sites to see, how to organize your day, which sites you need to register for in advance and some very useful tips. Before we get to the actual planning, I just want to say that in Jerusalem, much more than in Tel Aviv, it’s important that you sleep in the city center. You’ve found a wonderful Airbnb in the Ramot neighborhood? Great! It’s not for you. Do yourself a favor and sleep in the center. In my post about accommodation in Jerusalem I give some great options for all budgets. The most important places in Jerusalem are in the Old City: the Western Wall, the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, the Dome of the Rock, the Via Dolorosa, the Jewish quarter... There are two areas that are located outside the Old City but are still part of it and they are usually visited together. The first is Mount Zion, where you will find the Tomb of King David, the Room of the Last Supper and the Dormition Abbey. And outside the Dung Gate, which is near the Western Wall, you have the City of David national park, which is ancient Jerusalem. This is a very interesting site: a large section of it is actually underground and you also have the option of walking in waist-high water. Most of the sites are open at all times. The Western Wall is open 24/7 and the Church of the Holy Sepulchre is open from very early in the morning. If you want to enter the Western Wall tunnels, where you get to see the entire length of the Western Wall, then you need to register in advance. You will be able to find links to all of the places I mention in this video in the post linked below. If you want to visit the Temple Mount, then you need to know that non-Muslims can only access it via one entrance, which is located between the Western Wall and the Dung Gate (you will see the wooden bridge). The Temple Mount is open every day except Friday, for three hours in the morning, and one hour at noon. Make sure you dress modestly and be aware that no knives or religious books are allowed. On the Temple Mount there are two monumental buildings: the Dome of the Rock, the most iconic building in Jerusalem, and the Al-Aqsa Mosque. Most people will visit the Old City more than once, as there is a lot to see and absorb and there are lots of shops and places to eat. If you’re sleeping in the city center, it’s easy to get to the Old City. I really recommend visiting the Old City early in the morning, especially if you want to visit the Church of the Holy Sepulchre. Sometimes I get thank-you emails from people from Wisconsin and the rest of the world, which I really appreciate. Anyway, the single tip that travelers mention the most is visiting the Church of the Holy Sepulchre early in the morning. If visiting the church is important to you, go early. At midday it is one big balagan, the Hebrew word for chaos. Another part of the city is the Mount of Olives. You can reach the Mount of Olives on foot by passing through the Lions’ Gate and then climbing the mountain (it is quite steep), or alternatively you can take a taxi. If you want to take a bus, you need to take it from the bus station outside of Damascus Gate. These are two main bus stations in Jerusalem: one in the west, next to the light rail station and central station and one on the Arabic side, next to Damascus Gate. From there you can also take a bus to Bethlehem. At the top of the Mount of Olives is the Chapel of the Ascension where, according to tradition, Jesus ascended to heaven. Many of the sights on the Mount of Olives are connected to Christianity: the Church of the Pater Noster, Dominus Flevit, the Church of Gethsemane and others. The reason that there are so many important churches is that Christianity tends to commemorate events connected to the life of Jesus by building churches, which is something that can’t be said of Judaism and Islam. But the sights on the Mount of Olives are not only of interest to practicing Christians. Mount of Olives is higher up than the Old City so the view is amazing. All the videos you can see about Jerusalem start with a 3-second shot from the Mount of Olives. Another significant location is the Jewish cemetery, one of the most important Jewish cemeteries in the world, since Jews have been buried here for around three thousand years. Allow yourself 2 to 3 hours to see the main sights. Unlike in the Old City, there are almost no places to eat while visiting the various points of interest. On hot days (or indeed always) make sure you have a bottle of water with you. In the city center there is also a lot to see. Many of the sites, or I guess I mean neighborhoods, are at or a few minutes’ walk from the light rail stations of City Hall, Jaffa Center and Machine Yehuda. There is Mea Shearim, which is the ultra-orthodox neighborhood, Mishkenot Sha'ananim, the first Jewish neighborhood outside the Old City, and Nachlaot, Machine Yehuda, which is the market. The nice thing about Jerusalem is that although it’s the biggest city in Israel, with almost one million inhabitants, the neighborhoods in the city center haven’t changed much in the last hundred years. There are hardly any skyscrapers. The last station on the light rail line is called Mount Herzl and it is on this mountain that you will find the grave of the father of modern Zionism, Theodor Herzl, the Great Leaders of the Nation’s Plot, the biggest military cemetery, with dozens of memorials, and Yad Vashem, also known as the Holocaust museum. Yad Vashem is one of the most visited sites in Israel, for good reason, yet only a few tourists visit Mount Herzl. I find it disappointing that many tours skip Mount Herzl. In my opinion, there is a discrepancy between where the tours take travelers to, and where the interest of the travelers lies. The mainstream tours visit Masada, Jaffa, Caesarea, Banias, Megiddo – all ancient sites, and almost no sites that are connected to the state of Israel. But what tourists tend to ask about is the modern state of Israel. And on Mount Herzl you have the perfect opportunity to learn about the state of Israel. There is a connecting path, which is literally called the connection path, from Yad Vashem to the memorials on Mount Herzl, and yet very few go there. I can’t understand why they don’t. Anyway, from the light rail station at Mount Herzl, you can take the bus to Ein Karem, a quiet village on the outskirts of the city. There are two important churches in this neighborhood: the Church of the Visitation and the Church of St John the Baptist. There are also lots of nice little spots where you can sit back and relax. And when visiting Jerusalem, you definitely need a little down time. Givat Ram is a neighborhood where many of the national institutions are located, among them the Supreme Court, the Bank of Israel, the National Library, and two sites that are relevant to travelers – the Knesset (the Israeli Parliament) and the Israel Museum, the most important museum in Israel. You can look at the Knesset from the outside or register in advance for a tour of the inside. In the post below you’ll be able to find all the details. Next to the Knesset stands Israel’s most impressive museum, the Israel Museum. Here you can see the Shrine of the Book, some of the oldest parts of the Bible that have ever been found, the largest and by far the most important collection of Israel’s archeological finds, and a whole wing concerned with Jewish life and culture. What I absolutely love about the Israel Museum is that it is the Israel Museum. There are many museums, like the British Museum in London, or the Pergamon in Berlin, where the important artifacts are not necessarily from the UK or Germany. And in the Israel Museum you will be able to see all the most significant artifacts that have been found in all the sites you will be visiting in Israel, be it Caesarea, Tel Dan, or Jerusalem. So the museum sums up all the sites in one place. The museum offers free tours and you can find the link below. So, those are the main sites. I would suggest the following itinerary. Day one: the Mount of Olives, the Old City and the city center. Day two: the Old City, Temple Mount, the City of David and the city center. Day three: Mount Herzl, city center and then the Israel Museum. A word about visiting Jerusalem with children. Too much archeology, religion and history is not easy to take – not for adults, and certainly not for children. Luckily, Jerusalem has more to offer than just holy sites. I’ll be writing a whole post on this subject, but for now the main attractions for children include the Bloomfield Science Museum, which is an excellent interactive museum. Then there is the Jerusalem Biblical Zoo, where the focus lies on the animals that once lived in Israel and those that are mentioned in the Bible. Also, the Israel Museum has a large wing dedicated to children. Although this is a video about 3 days in Jerusalem, I know that some people visiting Israel for a conference or on business might only have two days in Israel. If you have 48 hours in Israel, then I would recommend one day in Jerusalem for the Old City, the City of David and the market. And then a day tour to Masada and the Dead Sea. Getting from Jerusalem to Masada takes about an hour and a half and this is probably the most popular day tour in Israel. All the information about Masada and the Dead Sea will be in the post linked below. The most important thing is not just seeing the sites but understanding what you’re looking at. You can join a tour, you can find my recommendations on my website, or you can hire a private guide, like myself. Yes, I will soon be starting to guide in Jerusalem again. And if you are on a budget, you can purchase my app, Jerusalem with Oren, with all of my Jerusalem tours as videos, discount codes and tips. It will take your Jerusalem experience to a much higher level. This week I signed my mortgage – about a million shekels. Now I have done all the calculations, and I only need around 20,000 of you to purchase my app and then that’s it – no more mortgage, so do the right thing! That’s it, give this video a like and subscribe for more tips. All the important information will be in the link below. See you in Israel. Yalla bye.

recommendations: For all my tips, accommodation recommendations, digital tours and booklets, check out my internet site -
I really recommend visiting the Old City early in the morning, especially if you want to visit the Church of the Holy Sepulchre
At the top of the Mount of Olives is the Chapel of the Ascension where, according to tradition, Jesus ascended to heaven
If you have 48 hours in Israel, then I would recommend one day in Jerusalem for the Old City, the City of David and the market
You can join a tour, you can find my recommendations on my website, or you can hire a private guide, like myself

videos.title: Israel Marks Jerusalem’s Reunification; Advisory urges Israelis to avoid Turkey TV7Israel News 30.05
videos.description:
Today’s top stories 30.05.22;

1) Approximately 70,000 Israelis participate in an annual Flags Parade in Jerusalem to mark the 55th anniversary of the city’s reunification.
2) Israeli Prime Minister Naftali Bennett urges the international community to stand alongside the Iranian people amid a brutal crackdown perpetrated by Revolutionary forces loyal to the Ayatollah regime.
3) Israel’s National Security Council’s Counter-Terrorism Bureau publishes a travel advisory warning Israelis against traveling to Turkey amid Iranian efforts to attack Israeli targets around the world.

You are welcome to join our audience and watch all of our programs - free of charge! 

TV7 Israel News: https://www.tv7israelnews.com/vod/series/563/

Jerusalem Studio: https://www.tv7israelnews.com/vod/series/18738/ 

TV7 Israel News Editor’s Note: https://www.tv7israelnews.com/vod/series/76269/

TV7 Israel: Watchmen Talk: https://www.tv7israelnews.com/vod/series/76256/

Jerusalem Prays: https://www.tv7israelnews.com/vod/series/135790/

TV7’s Times Observer: https://www.tv7israelnews.com/vod/series/97531/

TV7’s Middle East Review: https://www.tv7israelnews.com/vod/series/997755/

My Brother’s Keeper: https://www.tv7israelnews.com/vod/series/53719/

This week in 60 seconds: https://www.tv7israelnews.com/vod/series/123456/

Those who wish can send prayer requests to TV7 Israel News in the following ways: 
Facebook Messenger: https://www.facebook.com/tv7israelnews 
Email: israelnews@tv7.fi 
Please be sure to mention your first name and country of residence. Any attached videos should not exceed 20 seconds in duration.

#IsraelNews #tv7israelnews #newsupdates

Rally behind our vision - https://www.tv7israelnews.com/donate/

Follow TV7 Israel News on Instagram - https://www.instagram.com/tv7israelnews/
Follow TV7 Israel News on Twitter - https://twitter.com/tv7israelnews
videos.transcript: Shalom and good evening, this is TV7 Israel News broadcast to you from Jerusalem. And in today’s top stories: Approximately 70,000 Israelis participate in an annual Flags Parade in Jerusalem to mark the 55th anniversary of the city’s reunification. Israeli Prime Minister Naftali Bennett urges the international community to stand alongside the Iranian people amid a brutal crackdown perpetrated by Revolutionary forces loyal to the Ayatollah regime. Israel’s National Security Council’s Counter-Terrorism Bureau publishes a travel advisory warning Israelis against traveling to Turkey amid Iranian efforts to attack Israeli targets around the world. Approximately 70,000 Israelis participated in an annual Flags Parade in Jerusalem yesterday to mark the 55th anniversary of the city’s reunification. Ahead of the celebratory parade, the Islamist Hamas and the Iranian-proxies Palestinian Islamic Jihad and Hezbollah levelled separate threats toward the Jewish State - vowing to launch barrages of rockets at Israel if the sites, which are regarded as holy to Islam, would be desecrated. Nevertheless, while maintain vigilance, Israel’s defense establishment regarded the voiced rhetoric as hollow of substance. Meanwhile, speaking at a state memorial for fallen IDF troops that participated in the Six Day War, which culminated with the liberation of Jerusalem; Defense Minister Benny Gantz quoted the late Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin, who served as the IDF Chief of General Staff during the multisector war, highlighting its relevance for today. “I felt the sources of power, both moral and spiritual, of the IDF – the army of a nation that wants peace yet knows to fight fiercely when war is forced upon it. This is an army that unveils the good and beauty of the nation at times of distress and emergency; this is an army that shows unparalleled combat capability all-the-while safeguarding, also during the storm of battle, its human form.” Essential words that remain true for today, fifty-five years later. Yes, also today, we want peace and know to fight fiercely if war is forced upon us. And also in our war on terror we safeguard our human form. And also today, here in Jerusalem, we remain determined that everyone will have the right to live in security.” Earlier in the day, the Israeli cabinet held a celebratory meeting, in which it marked the reunification and consequent assertion of Israeli sovereignty over Jerusalem. “55 years ago, IDF soldiers liberated the Old City and unified Jerusalem. This was an event that in one moment united the entire nation. Today we mark Jerusalem Day and during the day there will be many events, including the traditional flag parade that will proceed along the regular route. Flying the Israeli flag in the capital of Israel is self-evident; therefore, we have been crystal clear on the subject from the outset. I request that the participants celebrate responsibly and in a respectful manner. From here, on behalf of the ministers in the Government of Israel, I would like to thank the Israel Police for securing all of the events.” While the vast majority of those participating in the flags parade did so peacefully; Regrettably, clashes erupted in a number of flashpoints, which Police sources said, ‘were instigated by Islamist activists and two radical Jewish organizations, namely Lehava and La Familia.’ As a result, thirteen people were lightly-injured and material damaged was caused. Moreover, more than 60 suspects were arrested by Police forces during various incidents for alleged rioting, hurling of stones and for physical assault, all of which took place on the Temple Mount, as well as in other parts of the Old City and in adjacent East Jerusalem neighborhoods. Returning to the celebratory cabinet meeting in Jerusalem, where; Premier Naftali Bennett also seized the opportunity to highlight events taking place in the Islamic Republic – where the Ayatollah regime is seemingly trying to brutally quell anti-regime protests that are spreading throughout Iran. “This week we again saw that instead of improving the lives of the Iranian people, the regime in Tehran chooses tyranny, terror and lies. Last week masses of Iranian people went out into the streets of Abadan and other places in Iran to demonstrate against the tyranny of the regime. In response, Iranian forces shot at Iranian civilians. In many places in Iran at the moment it is difficult to acquire water and bread at a reasonable price. Iran invests in terror – like the underground UAV base they showcased recently. Iran has also been investing in lies such as its deliberate misleading of the IAEA in order to evade visits by the agency, as was revealed last week. The Iranian regime is based on tyranny, terror and lies.” It is important to note that the violent crackdown on protests in Iran by Tehran’s revolutionary authorities is astoundingly faced by ‘deafening silence’ from the international community. Separately, while Iranian civilians are once again faced with violent methods of oppression; Israel has published a travel advisory, warning its citizens against traveling to Turkey. In a statement by the National Security Council Counter Terrorism Bureau, “For several weeks, especially since Iran has accused Israel (of bearing responsibility for) the death of an IRGC officer last week, there is increasing concern in the security establishment regarding Iranian efforts to attack Israeli targets around the world. Therefore, the National Security Council and the security establishment underscore the travel warning to Turkey and reiterate that it is a country that currently has a high level of risk for Israelis.” Moreover, according to the security establishment, the warning stems from a tangible threat to Israelis in Turkey. There is also a higher threat level in additional countries bordering Iran. Therefore, Israeli citizens should be alert and take care to show all due caution regarding travel to any of these countries. And while Israeli citizens are urged to exercise extreme caution; the Israeli Defense Force continue to prepare for all-out-war – as part of the “Chariots of Fire” exercise. Upon conclusion of the first three weeks of the multi-sector exercise; The IDF highlighted the unique aspect of this wide-scale exercise, which includes a string of unprecedented methodologies that are being implemented. “Over these past three weeks the Division simulated separate emergency scenarios, with chief focus on the northern sector, with an upmost challenge that was centered by a (ground) maneuver. The maneuver is a significant and important tool during which we are tested first-and-foremost on our ability to fulfill our missions within pre-planned timetables all the while displaying new methods including intelligence that is transmitted to the force and employment of aerial activity of significant magnitude by the fighting forces.” Colonel L, Head of the IDF’s Field Intelligence Department, explains more on the newly integrated methodology, which includes a unique live-time intelligence update for the IDF’s operational forces on the frontlines and beyond, effectively granting them the capacity to anticipate any enemy movements prior, during and following hostile engagements. “The Field Intelligence Department, in cooperation with all of the Intelligence Directorate Units, Land Forces Branch and additional branches, acted over the past year to make information available at the Intelligence Directorate accessible to the operational units. As part of this, we spearheaded a number of significant projects. The first includes the erection of a maneuver and defense center, an intelligence center that incorporates within it all of the representatives of the Intelligence Directorate, from which all available intelligence is formulated in accordance with the needs and questions of the Separate Divisions and Corps and to make it accessible to the operational front-line’ forces. This center is unique and a first of its kind and is being incorporated and drilled during the current exercise (Chariots of Fire).” Next to the newly implemented live-time intelligence updates for IDF troops on the ground; Israel underwent in recent years a robust logistical shift in which alternate roads have been paved for times of emergency – when a conflict on multiple fronts will ultimately erupt. “In the backdrop a plan was formulated, per the Chief of General Staff’s directives, “the route of routes”, which aims to provide an alternative to blockage in roads in the State of Israel and enables to safely transport significant operational manpower from the Home Front to the Front in all of the sectors and the umbrella of a digital system that enables it. This was exercised significantly in the past several weeks, with many lessons learned which will be taken and expanded.” It is important to note that these are merely a couple newly integrated methodologies which the IDF approved-for-publication – as it has integrated dozens of additional technological systems into its war-plans for an increasingly probable multi-sector conflict vis-à-vis the Islamic Republic of Iran and its regional proxies and partners. Thank you for watching TV7 Israel News. TV7 Israel is a donation-based ministry. Therefore, if you are blessed by our productions – please consider making a financial contribution that in-turn will enable us to sustain our ongoing operations. Separately, I would like to encourage you - pray for our persecuted brothers and sisters worldwide, in addition to unceasing prayers for the peace of Jerusalem and the Salvation of Israel. I’m Jonathan Hessen,

safetyNotes: Israel Marks Jerusalem’s Reunification; Advisory urges Israelis to avoid Turkey TV7Israel News 30
Israel’s National Security Council’s Counter-Terrorism Bureau publishes a travel advisory warning Israelis against traveling to Turkey amid Iranian efforts to attack Israeli targets around the world
Separately, while Iranian civilians are once again faced with violent methods of oppression; Israel has published a travel advisory, warning its citizens against traveling to Turkey
Therefore, the National Security Council and the security establishment underscore the travel warning to Turkey and reiterate that it is a country that currently has a high level of risk for Israelis
” Moreover, according to the security establishment, the warning stems from a tangible threat to Israelis in Turkey
Therefore, Israeli citizens should be alert and take care to show all due caution regarding travel to any of these countries
And while Israeli citizens are urged to exercise extreme caution; the Israeli Defense Force continue to prepare for all-out-war – as part of the “Chariots of Fire” exercise
recommendations: Today’s top stories 30
And in today’s top stories: Approximately 70,000 Israelis participate in an annual Flags Parade in Jerusalem to mark the 55th anniversary of the city’s reunification

videos.title: Tel Aviv in 5 minutes | Travel Guide | With Day Trip to Jerusalem
videos.description:
IMPORTANT NOTICE - please read first, then comment!!!

Over a year has passed since we visited Tel Aviv - a city full of life, culture, diversity and creativity. But shortly after our trip, everything changed. The war in Israel brought suffering, uncertainty and fear. During this time, it didn't seem right to show carefree travelling moments when so many people were facing pain and loss.

Nevertheless, after careful consideration, we decided to publish our video now. Not because we ignore the difficult reality, but because we believe it is important to show the other side: the vibrant, open and inspiring Tel Aviv. A city where people of different backgrounds, religions and lifestyles come together to live, create and enjoy together.

This video is a glimpse of the Tel Aviv and Israel we experienced - full of joie de vivre, hospitality and creativity. It is a sign of hope and a reminder that behind the headlines are real people and places that are more than a conflict.

We know that the situation remains complex and painful, and we do not want to trivialise suffering or take political positions. Our aim is to show a piece of the beauty and diversity that make up this city and this country - and perhaps to remind people of the importance of understanding and humanity in difficult times.

Thank you for walking this path with us.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
Original text (from September 2023):

Tel Aviv. A colorful, lively city between tradition and modernity. A hotspot for young people and cosmopolitan travelers. With 300 days of sunshine a year, a great vacation is guaranteed here. Tel Aviv in 5 minutes is a travel guide including the beste attractions and a day trip to Jerusalem.
► Important information: Video was recorded in September 2023.

At Carmel Market, you can find souvenirs for your family and friends back home. Also sweet mountains of halva, baklava and kadaifi, colorful fruit and juice stands, spice vendors and a variety of street kitchens. We continue through the artists' quarter Florentin to the old town of Jaffa. It really is very beautiful. You can get an impression of the history and culture of Israel on a walk. 

The coast of Tel Aviv stretches for 14 kilometers with its numerous beaches. Hilton Beach is particularly popular with the rainbow community. Tel Aviv has generally made a point of being particularly LGBT-friendly. Gay Pride is also celebrated here every year.

We take a day trip to Jerusalem. It has the highest density of religious buildings in the world. The Church of the Holy Sepulchre is one of the largest shrines of Christianity. Jesus is said to have been crucified and buried here. Jews from all over the world come to the Wailing Wall to pray and commemorate. And on the Temple Mount is the Al-Aqsa Mosque, which is important for Muslims.

The next day, we take a day trip from Tel Aviv in a rental car. Driving on highways 1 and 90 through the West Bank is generally permitted, but this can change at any time. Our first destination: Masada National Park. According to legend, Herod built a palace here more than 2000 years ago. Nearby is the Dead Sea, the lowest point on earth.

We recommend a 5-7 day trip to Tel Aviv, if it is safe to travel to Tel Aviv. You can book your apartment here: https://bit.ly/Tel-Aviv-Trip * 

#theTravellers #israel #telaviv #travelbloggers

videos.title: Busting Myths about Jerusalem: The Real Marketplace?
videos.description:
Join Doron and Franny as they discover Jerusalem and bust some archaeological myths along the way!

How old is the Jerusalem shuk or market? What did people use as the spot to do their shopping in Jerusalem? What are some famous spots in Jerusalem that we mistook for something else? Where do you do your shopping when you come to Jerusalem?

Credit: Amit Ben Atar, COD Archive

#jerusalem #cityofdavid #archaeology #finds #visitjerusalem #visitisrael #israel #traveljerusalem #excavation #excavations #artifacts #artifact #romanempire #romanartifact #kingdavid #tempele #davidbeeri #davidbeery
videos.transcript: [Music] guys welcome to the shook the market you know this is amazing i feel like we're in diagon alley from harry potter totally i mean there's colors and smells like spices and fabrics it's so vibrant really there's nothing you can't find in the shook franny i feel like if i got into a time machine and went back in time it would look exactly the same yeah i mean it is pretty ancient dates back to parts of it date back to the byzantine empire but sorry guys just like everything we're talking about today it's not the original so what was the original ship used for well the original market actually were for people who were going up to the temple it was an ascent and we've been going down in fact the western wall is that way so this goes down they used to go up so you're telling me the shook's not the shook the tower is not the original tower and the gate's not the original gate franny throw us a lifeline here where are we well actually i think that in order to but these were practical needs you had to have a story to buy the things you actually needed that's right i mean i guess we've uncovered it this is the original road this is the original shook from
